summary:
During dental tipping, different parts of the periodontal ligament experience both stretching and compression due to uneven stress distribution; thus, resulting in partially stretched and compressed fiber segments within it.
The periodontal ligament undergoes complex mechanical changes with tooth tilting, leading to selective tensioning or compaction among various fibrous components based on their positioning along the tilted axis. To summarize concisely for your request: Tipping causes certain PDL fibers to become more extended whereas other sections get compacted, reflecting an overall mixed state where not all fibers are uniformly affected but rather exhibit localized variations between being fully elongated versus squashed under differing levels of force application across these specific anatomical regions subjected directly towards such displacements from vertical alignment postures originally held prior initiating any rotational movement patterns observed clinically here today regarding patient care considerations involving orthodontic treatments aimed at achieving desired aesthetic outcomes through controlled manipulation techniques applied strategically over time utilizing appropriate appliances designed specifically targeting those particular teeth requiring adjustments as part broader treatment plans addressing malocclusions etc... 

To meet exactly two sentence requirement without repetition above summary would read like so instead : 
Tipping induces varied mechanical responses throughout the periodontal ligaments , creating stretches alongside compressions amongst constituent collagenous strands . This leads to non-uniform deformation states wherein selected areas may extend whilst adjacent ones contract accordingly according orientation dictated primarily via external loads imposed durably
